cards.hook: A mantis shrimp punch can make the water flash.
cards.core_fact: Smasher mantis shrimp strikes can create cavitation bubbles; collapse of those bubbles adds a second impact.

beats.voiceover: A mantis shrimp punch can make the water flash.
beats.caption: A mantis shrimp punch can make the water flash
beats.visual: photoreal macro shrimp impact with cavitation bubbles and shock ripple

beats.voiceover: The animal is tiny, but its club moves so fast that the water briefly tears open into bubbles.
beats.caption: the animal is tiny / but its club moves insanely fast
beats.visual: tiny mantis shrimp portrait followed by club/impact macro

beats.voiceover: That is called cavitation.
beats.caption: that is cavitation
beats.visual: bubble cloud forming beside impact, no diagram labels

beats.voiceover: The punch hits first.
beats.caption: the punch hits first
beats.visual: club reaching shell/prey proxy, clear first impact

beats.voiceover: Then the bubble collapses and hits again.
beats.caption: then the bubble collapses / and hits again
beats.visual: collapsing bubble cloud/shock ripple near target

beats.voiceover: Scientists filmed this shock in high speed, because the second hit happens too fast for our eyes.
beats.caption: scientists filmed it / in high speed
beats.visual: high-speed macro sequence/contact-sheet-like visual without UI/readouts

beats.voiceover: So the mantis shrimp does not just punch prey.
beats.caption: it does not just / punch prey
beats.visual: shrimp poised after strike, educational wonder not scary

beats.voiceover: It punches the water so hard the water punches too.
beats.caption: the water / punches too
beats.visual: final bubble-collapse ripple around shrimp/impact

sources: Nature: Deadly strike mechanism of a mantis shrimp, DOI 10.1038/428819a, https://www.nature.com/articles/428819a
Journal of Experimental Biology: Elastic Energy Powers Mantis Shrimp Punch, DOI 10.1242/jeb.040691, https://doi.org/10.1242/jeb.040691
Bioinspiration & Biomimetics: A physical model of the extreme mantis shrimp strike, DOI 10.1088/1748-3182/9/1/016014

cards.core_fact: A giant hexagon-shaped jet stream wraps around Saturn's north pole, with storms and a polar vortex inside.

sources: NASA/JPL PIA21052 Over Saturns Turbulent North
NASA/JPL PIA17122 Stormy North

beats.caption: Saturn has a hexagon storm

cards.core_fact: Wombats are the only known animals that produce cube-shaped scat; researchers link the shape to how the intestine stretches and moves.
cards.source_policy: Wikimedia/public-source wombat photos plus tiny local object overlay; no paid credits.
sources: Soft Matter: Intestines of non-uniform stiffness mold the corners of wombat feces, DOI 10.1039/D0SM01230K
BBC/Nature coverage of wombat cube feces research, source for public explanation only

beats.voiceover: Wombats poop cubes.
beats.caption: Wombats poop cubes
beats.visual: real wombat in grass, cute not childish, instant hook

beats.voiceover: Not perfect toy blocks, but little cube-shaped pellets.
beats.caption: little cube shaped pellets
beats.visual: macro-safe tiny cube scat proxy beside natural ground

beats.voiceover: Scientists think the shape comes from uneven stretching inside the intestine.
beats.caption: uneven stretching / inside the intestine
beats.visual: non-text anatomical motion proxy, no diagram labels

beats.voiceover: The corners form before the poop ever lands.
beats.caption: the corners form first
beats.visual: process visual, avoid fake labels

beats.voiceover: So the weirdest bathroom fact in nature is also physics.
beats.caption: bathroom fact / plus physics
beats.visual: wombat walking away, family-friendly final beat

cards.core_fact: Two octopus hearts pump blood through the gills, while a third pumps blood to the rest of the body.
cards.source_policy: Public-source/octopus reference photos; no paid credits; avoid fake anatomy in video lane.
sources: Smithsonian Ocean: Octopus facts and biology overview
Encyclopaedia Britannica: cephalopod circulatory system overview

beats.voiceover: An octopus has three hearts.
beats.caption: An octopus has three hearts
beats.visual: beautiful real octopus, calm full-body read

beats.voiceover: Two hearts push blood through the gills.
beats.caption: two hearts / push through the gills
beats.visual: two subtle pulse points near gill area, no labels

beats.voiceover: The third sends blood to the rest of the body.
beats.caption: the third / powers the body
beats.visual: single subtle body pulse, no diagram text

beats.voiceover: And when it swims, that body heart can slow down.
beats.caption: when it swims / one heart slows
beats.visual: octopus swimming motion, avoid detached tentacles

beats.voiceover: That is why crawling can be easier than swimming.
beats.caption: crawling can be easier
beats.visual: octopus moving along seafloor, anatomy-safe

cards.core_fact: When conditions are extreme, tardigrades can curl into a dry tun state and slow their metabolism dramatically until water returns.
cards.source_policy: Public microscope imagery plus motion graphics; no paid credits.
sources: NASA: Tardigrades and space survival research overview
Current Biology / peer-reviewed tardigrade cryptobiosis literature for fact wording

beats.voiceover: Tardigrades can pause their bodies.
beats.caption: Tardigrades can pause their bodies
beats.visual: microscope tardigrade close-up, cute but real

beats.voiceover: When things get too dry, they curl into a tiny tun.
beats.caption: too dry / tiny tun
beats.visual: curling body shape or before-after microscope proxy

beats.voiceover: Their metabolism drops so low it is almost like waiting on pause.
beats.caption: metabolism drops / like pause
beats.visual: stillness with subtle heartbeat/motion graphic, no text

beats.voiceover: Then when water comes back, some can wake up again.
beats.caption: water returns / they wake up
beats.visual: rehydration motion proxy, microscope look

beats.voiceover: It is one of nature's strangest survival tricks.
beats.caption: nature's strangest / survival trick
beats.visual: final tardigrade hero macro/microscope look

cards.core_fact: Wood frogs survive winter by letting much of their body freeze while glucose-rich fluids help protect cells until thawing.
cards.source_policy: Public-source real wood frog imagery and simple freeze/thaw motion graphics; no paid credits.
sources: National Park Service wood frog freeze tolerance overview
Peer-reviewed wood frog freeze tolerance literature for fact wording

beats.voiceover: A frog can freeze solid.
beats.caption: A frog can freeze solid
beats.visual: real wood frog on leaf litter with icy edge, instant animal read

beats.voiceover: In winter, ice can form through much of its body.
beats.caption: ice forms through its body
beats.visual: macro frost creeping around frog body, no horror, no labels

beats.voiceover: Its heart can stop beating for a while.
beats.caption: its heart can stop
beats.visual: calm freeze-state close-up with subtle pulse fading, no medical UI

beats.voiceover: Sugar-like antifreeze helps protect the cells.
beats.caption: sugar helps protect cells
beats.visual: simple cellular protection visual without text or diagram labels

beats.voiceover: Then spring warms it up, and the frog starts moving again.
beats.caption: spring warms it / back to life
beats.visual: thawing frog on wet leaves, tiny visible movement

cards.hook: Some snakes can glide through the air.
cards.core_fact: Flying snakes flatten their bodies and undulate in the air, creating lift and control as they glide from trees.
cards.source_policy: Use clear real/source visuals or clean generated proof plates; no paid credits without approval.
sources: Nature Physics: Undulation enables gliding in flying snakes, https://www.nature.com/articles/s41567-020-0935-4

beats.voiceover: Some snakes can glide through the air.
beats.caption: Some snakes can glide through the air
beats.visual: snake launching from tree branch into open air, instantly readable

beats.voiceover: They do not have wings.
beats.caption: no wings
beats.visual: clear side view of snake body in air, no wings, tree background

beats.voiceover: They flatten their body like a long moving airfoil.
beats.caption: they flatten their body
beats.visual: body visibly wider/flatter, simple profile transformation with no labels

beats.voiceover: Then they wave side to side while falling forward.
beats.caption: they wave side to side
beats.visual: S-shaped snake gliding path through air

beats.voiceover: That motion helps them steer from one tree to another.
beats.caption: steering tree to tree
beats.visual: snake gliding toward another branch, clear destination

cards.hook: An archerfish can shoot insects out of the air.
cards.core_fact: Archerfish hunt by firing precise jets of water at insects above the surface; jet dynamics and aim help knock prey down.
cards.status_column: Source Acquisition Partial Pass - Missing Voice/Proof Gate
cards.source_policy: Use right-safe/public-source archerfish imagery or clean generated proof plates; no paid credits without approval.
sources: Proceedings of the Royal Society B / archerfish water-jet dynamics research overview
Current Biology / archerfish predictive shooting and hunting behavior research overview
Encyclopaedia Britannica / archerfish natural history overview

beats.voiceover: An archerfish can shoot insects out of the air.
beats.caption: An archerfish can shoot insects out of the air
beats.visual: clear fish at water surface aiming at insect above, instant water-jet hook

beats.voiceover: It makes a tiny water cannon with its mouth.
beats.caption: a tiny water cannon
beats.visual: close fish mouth at surface forming a narrow upward water jet

beats.voiceover: The jet gets stronger as the front catches up with the back.
beats.caption: the jet gets stronger
beats.visual: simple no-text slow-motion water stream thickening toward the target

beats.voiceover: Then the insect falls, and the fish grabs it.
beats.caption: the insect falls
beats.visual: insect knocked from leaf/branch toward water with fish below

beats.voiceover: So this little fish hunts by doing physics.
beats.caption: it hunts with physics
beats.visual: final full-frame fish surfacing with splash/ripples, family-friendly wonder
